channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: The Renters’ Rights Act’s Quiet Second Act: Why 2026 Turns the Checkout Into a Compliance Record
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Renters' Reform Act has already transformed the private rental sector with its headline changes in 2026, including the abolition of Section 21 evictions and new restrictions on rent increases. However, many landlords and tenants may not yet have noticed the quieter but equally significant compliance requirements now affecting the end-of-tenancy process.

How Checkout Reports Became Legal Documents

Whilst much attention focused on the initial reforms, the legislation introduced meticulous record-keeping obligations that take full effect this year. Inventory clerks and letting agents now face stricter requirements when compiling checkout reports, which have effectively become compliance documents rather than simple condition assessments.

Key changes include:

  • Mandatory photographic evidence with timestamp and geolocation data
  • Standardised damage classification scales replacing subjective descriptions
  • Full disclosure of all deposit deductions with itemised cost breakdowns
  • Digital submission to a central government portal within seven working days

What This Means for Landlords and Tenants

For landlords, these changes create additional administrative responsibilities. Many are choosing to work with accredited inventory clerks to ensure full compliance, as failure to meet the new standards could jeopardise deposit disputes. The requirement for digital submission also means landlords need to maintain proper records throughout the tenancy.

Tenants benefit from greater transparency in the deposit protection process, but may need to be more diligent when:

  • Documenting the property's condition at move-in
  • Requesting copies of all check-in and checkout reports
  • Challenging any deductions that don't meet the new evidential standards

As the rental market adapts to these changes, both landlords and tenants may wish to familiarise themselves with the updated processes to ensure smooth tenancy transitions and avoid unnecessary disputes.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Renters' Reform Act has already transformed the private rental sector with its headline changes in 2026, including the abolition of Section 21 evictions and new restrictions on rent increases. However, many landlords and tenants may not yet have noticed the quieter but equally significant compliance requirements now affecting the end-of-tenancy process.

How Checkout Reports Became Legal Documents

Whilst much attention focused on the initial reforms, the legislation introduced meticulous record-keeping obligations that take full effect this year. Inventory clerks and letting agents now face stricter requirements when compiling checkout reports, which have effectively become compliance documents rather than simple condition assessments.

Key changes include:

  • Mandatory photographic evidence with timestamp and geolocation data
  • Standardised damage classification scales replacing subjective descriptions
  • Full disclosure of all deposit deductions with itemised cost breakdowns
  • Digital submission to a central government portal within seven working days

What This Means for Landlords and Tenants

For landlords, these changes create additional administrative responsibilities. Many are choosing to work with accredited inventory clerks to ensure full compliance, as failure to meet the new standards could jeopardise deposit disputes. The requirement for digital submission also means landlords need to maintain proper records throughout the tenancy.

Tenants benefit from greater transparency in the deposit protection process, but may need to be more diligent when:

  • Documenting the property's condition at move-in
  • Requesting copies of all check-in and checkout reports
  • Challenging any deductions that don't meet the new evidential standards

As the rental market adapts to these changes, both landlords and tenants may wish to familiarise themselves with the updated processes to ensure smooth tenancy transitions and avoid unnecessary disputes.

item.title: Property industry reacts to Bank of England’s interest rate decision
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Bank of England Holds Interest Rates at 3.75%

The Bank of England has maintained interest rates at 3.75% for the fourth consecutive meeting, as policymakers continue to navigate inflation concerns against a fragile economic landscape. This widely anticipated decision leaves mortgage borrowers and homebuyers facing no immediate changes to borrowing costs, offering a temporary reprieve amidst ongoing affordability pressures.

Inflation remains stubbornly above the Bank’s 2% target, though recent economic data has been less severe than feared, despite geopolitical tensions affecting global energy prices. The Monetary Policy Committee voted 7-2 in favour of holding rates, with two members advocating for an increase to 4%. The housing market, while subdued, may benefit from this period of stability as buyers and sellers assess their options.

Industry Reaction: Resilience Amid Uncertainty

Property professionals have noted the market’s resilience in the face of higher borrowing costs and economic uncertainty. Mark Manning, managing director of Northern Estate Agencies Group, commented: "The property market has shown remarkable stability despite recent challenges. While buyer confidence hasn’t fully recovered from peak affordability levels, demand persists where pricing is realistic."

He added: "Today’s decision may reassure those planning their next move, but sentiment is unlikely to shift dramatically overnight. The market’s continued stability should offer comfort to both buyers and sellers."

Matt Smith, Rightmove’s mortgage spokesperson, highlighted the short-term certainty this decision provides: "With the Base Rate held steady, movers gain some breathing room. While global events remain a factor, the immediate pressure for mortgage rate increases has eased."

What Comes Next?

Attention now turns to whether inflation continues to ease, potentially paving the way for rate cuts later in the year. For now, homeowners may wish to review their mortgage options, particularly those nearing the end of fixed-term deals. Borrowers might consider speaking to a broker to explore competitive rates amidst this period of relative stability.

While the housing market adjusts to the new normal of higher borrowing costs, today’s decision underscores the Bank’s cautious approach—balancing inflation control with the need to support economic growth.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Bank of England Holds Interest Rates at 3.75%

The Bank of England has maintained interest rates at 3.75% for the fourth consecutive meeting, as policymakers continue to navigate inflation concerns against a fragile economic landscape. This widely anticipated decision leaves mortgage borrowers and homebuyers facing no immediate changes to borrowing costs, offering a temporary reprieve amidst ongoing affordability pressures.

Inflation remains stubbornly above the Bank’s 2% target, though recent economic data has been less severe than feared, despite geopolitical tensions affecting global energy prices. The Monetary Policy Committee voted 7-2 in favour of holding rates, with two members advocating for an increase to 4%. The housing market, while subdued, may benefit from this period of stability as buyers and sellers assess their options.

Industry Reaction: Resilience Amid Uncertainty

Property professionals have noted the market’s resilience in the face of higher borrowing costs and economic uncertainty. Mark Manning, managing director of Northern Estate Agencies Group, commented: "The property market has shown remarkable stability despite recent challenges. While buyer confidence hasn’t fully recovered from peak affordability levels, demand persists where pricing is realistic."

He added: "Today’s decision may reassure those planning their next move, but sentiment is unlikely to shift dramatically overnight. The market’s continued stability should offer comfort to both buyers and sellers."

Matt Smith, Rightmove’s mortgage spokesperson, highlighted the short-term certainty this decision provides: "With the Base Rate held steady, movers gain some breathing room. While global events remain a factor, the immediate pressure for mortgage rate increases has eased."

What Comes Next?

Attention now turns to whether inflation continues to ease, potentially paving the way for rate cuts later in the year. For now, homeowners may wish to review their mortgage options, particularly those nearing the end of fixed-term deals. Borrowers might consider speaking to a broker to explore competitive rates amidst this period of relative stability.

While the housing market adjusts to the new normal of higher borrowing costs, today’s decision underscores the Bank’s cautious approach—balancing inflation control with the need to support economic growth.

item.title: From viewings to best-seller – estate agent tops Amazon charts
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A Chester-based estate agent has claimed the top spot in Amazon’s property guides category with his latest book, offering homeowners practical strategies for navigating today’s competitive housing market. David Adams, managing director of Cavendish, saw The Seller’s Playbook reach number one in property guides and number two in finance and mortgages shortly after its release – mirroring the success of his previous best-seller, The Landlord’s Playbook.

Practical guidance for sellers

Drawing on 25 years of industry experience, Adams’ new publication aims to help homeowners avoid common pitfalls in the sales process. The book covers everything from accurate pricing and effective property presentation to negotiation tactics and completion strategies. With recent data showing only 53% of listed properties ultimately sell, Adams emphasises the importance of thorough preparation in a market where buyers have increasing choice.

“Today’s property landscape demands more strategic thinking from sellers,” Adams explains. “With greater competition and price sensitivity, simply listing a home isn’t enough – homeowners need to understand every stage of the process to make informed decisions.” The book combines market analysis with practical, tested approaches drawn from real transactions.

Why preparation matters

Adams highlights that for many individuals, selling a home represents one of their most significant financial decisions, yet research suggests many approach it without adequate preparation. The Seller’s Playbook seeks to address this gap by providing evidence-based guidance to help homeowners:

  • Set realistic pricing strategies from the outset
  • Enhance property appeal to attract serious buyers
  • Navigate the offer and negotiation process effectively
  • Avoid reactive decisions that could compromise outcomes

“The consequences of an unsuccessful sale can ripple through people’s lives, affecting relocation plans, financial situations and future property aspirations,” Adams notes. His book aims to equip sellers with the knowledge to take greater control of the process rather than relying solely on estate agents.

For homeowners considering a sale in the current market, industry-focused publications like Adams’ may offer valuable insights into evolving buyer expectations and effective sales techniques. While individual circumstances vary, understanding market dynamics could help sellers make more informed decisions about their property journey.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A Chester-based estate agent has claimed the top spot in Amazon’s property guides category with his latest book, offering homeowners practical strategies for navigating today’s competitive housing market. David Adams, managing director of Cavendish, saw The Seller’s Playbook reach number one in property guides and number two in finance and mortgages shortly after its release – mirroring the success of his previous best-seller, The Landlord’s Playbook.

Practical guidance for sellers

Drawing on 25 years of industry experience, Adams’ new publication aims to help homeowners avoid common pitfalls in the sales process. The book covers everything from accurate pricing and effective property presentation to negotiation tactics and completion strategies. With recent data showing only 53% of listed properties ultimately sell, Adams emphasises the importance of thorough preparation in a market where buyers have increasing choice.

“Today’s property landscape demands more strategic thinking from sellers,” Adams explains. “With greater competition and price sensitivity, simply listing a home isn’t enough – homeowners need to understand every stage of the process to make informed decisions.” The book combines market analysis with practical, tested approaches drawn from real transactions.

Why preparation matters

Adams highlights that for many individuals, selling a home represents one of their most significant financial decisions, yet research suggests many approach it without adequate preparation. The Seller’s Playbook seeks to address this gap by providing evidence-based guidance to help homeowners:

  • Set realistic pricing strategies from the outset
  • Enhance property appeal to attract serious buyers
  • Navigate the offer and negotiation process effectively
  • Avoid reactive decisions that could compromise outcomes

“The consequences of an unsuccessful sale can ripple through people’s lives, affecting relocation plans, financial situations and future property aspirations,” Adams notes. His book aims to equip sellers with the knowledge to take greater control of the process rather than relying solely on estate agents.

For homeowners considering a sale in the current market, industry-focused publications like Adams’ may offer valuable insights into evolving buyer expectations and effective sales techniques. While individual circumstances vary, understanding market dynamics could help sellers make more informed decisions about their property journey.

item.title: Number of interest-only mortgages drops by nearly a fifth in a year
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The number of interest-only mortgages in the UK has dropped significantly over the past year, continuing a decade-long decline according to the latest industry figures. Data from UK Finance reveals a 17.7% reduction in pure interest-only loans during 2025, leaving just 445,000 such mortgages outstanding. The trend extends to part-and-part mortgages too, with these hybrid products decreasing by 10.3% to 156,000 remaining accounts.

A shrinking market with stronger positions

Since UK Finance began tracking this data in 2012, the interest-only mortgage market has contracted dramatically - falling 81% in volume and 65% in value. The most substantial reductions have occurred in higher loan-to-value (LTV) products, with loans above 75% LTV plummeting by 26.9% last year alone. These now represent just 4% of the interest-only market, compared to 36% over a decade ago.

James Tatch, head of analytics at UK Finance, noted: "The remaining interest-only book is in a far stronger position, with over two thirds of customers having a loan-to-value ratio below 50%. This provides more options if borrowers cannot immediately repay when their term ends."

Improved repayment planning and lender support

The data suggests borrowers are becoming more proactive about repayment strategies. The number of loans maturing by 2027 halved during 2025, dropping from 120,000 to 60,000. This indicates fewer homeowners are approaching their maturity date without having addressed how they'll repay the capital.

Lenders' communication strategies appear to be having a positive impact, with tailored support helping borrowers prepare for the end of their terms. Tatch explained: "The small number who don't repay immediately upon maturity remains very low, and most do repay in full within the following months."

For those with interest-only mortgages approaching maturity, contacting their lender at the earliest opportunity could be beneficial. Most providers offer various solutions, including extending the term, switching to repayment, or considering equity release options for eligible borrowers.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The number of interest-only mortgages in the UK has dropped significantly over the past year, continuing a decade-long decline according to the latest industry figures. Data from UK Finance reveals a 17.7% reduction in pure interest-only loans during 2025, leaving just 445,000 such mortgages outstanding. The trend extends to part-and-part mortgages too, with these hybrid products decreasing by 10.3% to 156,000 remaining accounts.

A shrinking market with stronger positions

Since UK Finance began tracking this data in 2012, the interest-only mortgage market has contracted dramatically - falling 81% in volume and 65% in value. The most substantial reductions have occurred in higher loan-to-value (LTV) products, with loans above 75% LTV plummeting by 26.9% last year alone. These now represent just 4% of the interest-only market, compared to 36% over a decade ago.

James Tatch, head of analytics at UK Finance, noted: "The remaining interest-only book is in a far stronger position, with over two thirds of customers having a loan-to-value ratio below 50%. This provides more options if borrowers cannot immediately repay when their term ends."

Improved repayment planning and lender support

The data suggests borrowers are becoming more proactive about repayment strategies. The number of loans maturing by 2027 halved during 2025, dropping from 120,000 to 60,000. This indicates fewer homeowners are approaching their maturity date without having addressed how they'll repay the capital.

Lenders' communication strategies appear to be having a positive impact, with tailored support helping borrowers prepare for the end of their terms. Tatch explained: "The small number who don't repay immediately upon maturity remains very low, and most do repay in full within the following months."

For those with interest-only mortgages approaching maturity, contacting their lender at the earliest opportunity could be beneficial. Most providers offer various solutions, including extending the term, switching to repayment, or considering equity release options for eligible borrowers.

item.title: Estate agent issues stark warning as home sales dry up
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Recent government figures reveal a concerning slowdown in UK property transactions, with sales volumes dropping to their lowest point in five years. Whilst house prices have shown relative stability, the number of completed sales has fallen by nearly 40% compared to 2021 levels, raising fresh questions about market health.

Transaction slump becomes key concern

Industry experts suggest the dramatic reduction in housing market activity now presents a more pressing challenge than price fluctuations. Simon Gerrard of Martyn Gerrard Estate Agents notes that whilst official data shows some annual improvement, these figures compare against an exceptionally weak period following the end of Stamp Duty relief. The underlying trend points to significant lost momentum in recent months.

Rightmove's latest report compounds these concerns, recording the largest June asking price decline in 14 years. This softening market coincides with dwindling buyer confidence, as economic uncertainty makes many hesitant about major financial commitments. The combination of high inflation, interest rate volatility and geopolitical instability appears to be creating a perfect storm for market stagnation.

Potential paths to recovery

Some industry observers suggest that improving global economic conditions could help restore housing market momentum. A resolution to key geopolitical tensions, for instance, might ease inflationary pressures and provide more certainty around borrowing costs. Such developments could encourage potential buyers to re-enter the market.

For homeowners considering a move, current conditions present both challenges and opportunities. Whilst selling may require more realistic pricing expectations, those looking to purchase might find less competition and greater room for negotiation. Mortgage borrowers could benefit from comparing products carefully, as lenders increasingly compete for a smaller pool of applicants.

The coming months will prove crucial for the housing market's direction. With transaction levels at such low levels, any improvement in economic stability could help unlock pent-up demand. However, without intervention or changed conditions, the current stagnation may persist.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Recent government figures reveal a concerning slowdown in UK property transactions, with sales volumes dropping to their lowest point in five years. Whilst house prices have shown relative stability, the number of completed sales has fallen by nearly 40% compared to 2021 levels, raising fresh questions about market health.

Transaction slump becomes key concern

Industry experts suggest the dramatic reduction in housing market activity now presents a more pressing challenge than price fluctuations. Simon Gerrard of Martyn Gerrard Estate Agents notes that whilst official data shows some annual improvement, these figures compare against an exceptionally weak period following the end of Stamp Duty relief. The underlying trend points to significant lost momentum in recent months.

Rightmove's latest report compounds these concerns, recording the largest June asking price decline in 14 years. This softening market coincides with dwindling buyer confidence, as economic uncertainty makes many hesitant about major financial commitments. The combination of high inflation, interest rate volatility and geopolitical instability appears to be creating a perfect storm for market stagnation.

Potential paths to recovery

Some industry observers suggest that improving global economic conditions could help restore housing market momentum. A resolution to key geopolitical tensions, for instance, might ease inflationary pressures and provide more certainty around borrowing costs. Such developments could encourage potential buyers to re-enter the market.

For homeowners considering a move, current conditions present both challenges and opportunities. Whilst selling may require more realistic pricing expectations, those looking to purchase might find less competition and greater room for negotiation. Mortgage borrowers could benefit from comparing products carefully, as lenders increasingly compete for a smaller pool of applicants.

The coming months will prove crucial for the housing market's direction. With transaction levels at such low levels, any improvement in economic stability could help unlock pent-up demand. However, without intervention or changed conditions, the current stagnation may persist.

item.title: Five property firms expelled from The Property Ombudsman
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Five Property Firms Expelled from Ombudsman Scheme Over Unpaid Awards

The Property Ombudsman (TPO) has removed five property businesses from its redress scheme after they failed to comply with compensation rulings. The expulsions follow investigations by TPO's independent Finance, Performance & Compliance Committee, which found the firms in breach of their membership obligations.

Details of the Expelled Firms

The affected companies span different sectors of the property industry:

  • Devon Block Management Limited - Ordered to pay £75 after shortcomings in communicating with a residents' management company director
  • Pro Investment Properties Ltd - Faced the largest award of £7,400 relating to a disputed property sourcing agreement
  • RPS Estates - Directed to pay £1,871.25 to a landlord over rent collection and complaint handling issues
  • Samuel Francis & Co - Required to pay £350 following tenant complaints about mould reports
  • We Sell Lets - Ordered to pay £150 concerning a property sourcing service complaint

Notable Case Highlights

The most substantial award involved Pro Investment Properties Ltd, based in Redcar. The firm was found to have used unclear terms in a property sourcing agreement, resulting in a client paying a £7,200 finder's fee for a purchase that ultimately fell through. The Ombudsman awarded the full fee plus £200 compensation for distress.

In another case, RPS Estates (trading as Property Management Services Limited) faced criticism over its handling of landlord communications and rent collection. The £1,871.25 award reflected both the financial impact and the firm's poor complaint resolution.

Devon Block Management's smaller £75 penalty demonstrates that even relatively minor service failures can lead to expulsion when firms refuse to comply with Ombudsman decisions.

These expulsions serve as a reminder that property professionals might wish to ensure they maintain transparent communication and fair business practices to avoid similar sanctions. The TPO scheme provides important consumer protections, and membership removal could potentially affect firms' ability to trade in certain sectors of the property market.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Five Property Firms Expelled from Ombudsman Scheme Over Unpaid Awards

The Property Ombudsman (TPO) has removed five property businesses from its redress scheme after they failed to comply with compensation rulings. The expulsions follow investigations by TPO's independent Finance, Performance & Compliance Committee, which found the firms in breach of their membership obligations.

Details of the Expelled Firms

The affected companies span different sectors of the property industry:

  • Devon Block Management Limited - Ordered to pay £75 after shortcomings in communicating with a residents' management company director
  • Pro Investment Properties Ltd - Faced the largest award of £7,400 relating to a disputed property sourcing agreement
  • RPS Estates - Directed to pay £1,871.25 to a landlord over rent collection and complaint handling issues
  • Samuel Francis & Co - Required to pay £350 following tenant complaints about mould reports
  • We Sell Lets - Ordered to pay £150 concerning a property sourcing service complaint

Notable Case Highlights

The most substantial award involved Pro Investment Properties Ltd, based in Redcar. The firm was found to have used unclear terms in a property sourcing agreement, resulting in a client paying a £7,200 finder's fee for a purchase that ultimately fell through. The Ombudsman awarded the full fee plus £200 compensation for distress.

In another case, RPS Estates (trading as Property Management Services Limited) faced criticism over its handling of landlord communications and rent collection. The £1,871.25 award reflected both the financial impact and the firm's poor complaint resolution.

Devon Block Management's smaller £75 penalty demonstrates that even relatively minor service failures can lead to expulsion when firms refuse to comply with Ombudsman decisions.

These expulsions serve as a reminder that property professionals might wish to ensure they maintain transparent communication and fair business practices to avoid similar sanctions. The TPO scheme provides important consumer protections, and membership removal could potentially affect firms' ability to trade in certain sectors of the property market.

item.title: New warning signs for housing market as sales fall and supply soars
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Housing Market Shifts as Supply Rises and Sales Slow

The UK property market is showing signs of change, with increasing housing stock giving buyers more choice whilst presenting challenges for estate agents. Recent data reveals the number of homes available has reached its highest level in ten years, whilst agreed sales have dipped noticeably. This shift suggests a cooling market where sellers may need to adjust expectations to secure transactions.

Key Market Trends Emerging

Property listings have risen by 2.7% compared to the same period last year, with nearly 794,000 homes entering the market in the first five months of 2026. However, sales agreed in May fell by 8.1% year-on-year, indicating weakening momentum. Whilst demand remains above 2023 levels, the growing supply of properties is creating a more competitive environment for sellers.

Average asking prices have dipped slightly by 1.2% to £441,400, though achieved sale prices have stayed relatively stable. This suggests homeowners may wish to price realistically from the outset rather than relying on later reductions. The increased choice for buyers could mean properties take longer to sell, with the average time from offer to exchange now stretching to 132 days – a week longer than last year.

What This Means for Buyers and Sellers

For prospective buyers, the current conditions may present opportunities to negotiate favourable terms, particularly as fall-through rates have improved slightly. However, those looking to sell might consider that:

  • Accurate initial pricing could be crucial to stand out in a busier market
  • Preparation for longer transaction times may help manage expectations
  • Flexibility on terms might help secure committed buyers

Industry experts note that whilst the market remains active, the balance is shifting. With stock levels continuing to rise and sales slowing, the coming months could test how well the market adapts to these changing conditions.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Housing Market Shifts as Supply Rises and Sales Slow

The UK property market is showing signs of change, with increasing housing stock giving buyers more choice whilst presenting challenges for estate agents. Recent data reveals the number of homes available has reached its highest level in ten years, whilst agreed sales have dipped noticeably. This shift suggests a cooling market where sellers may need to adjust expectations to secure transactions.

Key Market Trends Emerging

Property listings have risen by 2.7% compared to the same period last year, with nearly 794,000 homes entering the market in the first five months of 2026. However, sales agreed in May fell by 8.1% year-on-year, indicating weakening momentum. Whilst demand remains above 2023 levels, the growing supply of properties is creating a more competitive environment for sellers.

Average asking prices have dipped slightly by 1.2% to £441,400, though achieved sale prices have stayed relatively stable. This suggests homeowners may wish to price realistically from the outset rather than relying on later reductions. The increased choice for buyers could mean properties take longer to sell, with the average time from offer to exchange now stretching to 132 days – a week longer than last year.

What This Means for Buyers and Sellers

For prospective buyers, the current conditions may present opportunities to negotiate favourable terms, particularly as fall-through rates have improved slightly. However, those looking to sell might consider that:

  • Accurate initial pricing could be crucial to stand out in a busier market
  • Preparation for longer transaction times may help manage expectations
  • Flexibility on terms might help secure committed buyers

Industry experts note that whilst the market remains active, the balance is shifting. With stock levels continuing to rise and sales slowing, the coming months could test how well the market adapts to these changing conditions.

item.title: Property market ‘weakening’ ahead of summer
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

UK House Prices Show Strongest Annual Growth Since 2025

UK property prices recorded their most robust annual increase in over a year during April, rising by 3.8% compared to the same period in 2025, according to the Office for National Statistics. This follows stagnant growth in March and marks the fastest rate of house price inflation since March 2025, just before alterations to Stamp Duty thresholds took effect. The average UK home now stands at £270,000, with regional variations painting a mixed picture of the market.

The North East emerged as the strongest performer, with values climbing 9.9% year-on-year, whilst London continued its downward trend as the weakest region. Property prices in the capital fell by 2.1% over the year to April, extending its run of annual declines to nine consecutive months. Across the nations, average prices reached £291,000 in England, £212,000 in Wales and £192,000 in Scotland. Northern Ireland saw prices rise to £198,000 in Q1 2026 – a 7.4% annual increase.

Experts suggest these figures reflect comparisons with last year's turbulent market conditions during Stamp Duty changes, which temporarily suppressed price growth. Monthly data shows a 0.7% uplift between March and April 2026.

Market Shifts Create Buyer Opportunities

Industry analysts note the current climate presents advantages for prospective purchasers. Richard Donnell of Zoopla observes: "The sales market is softening as we approach summer, with buyer demand 14% lower than this time last year, yet inventory levels remain higher." He emphasises that "serious sellers need to price competitively in what has become a buyers' market," with the North-South divide in pricing and activity persisting.

The rental market tells a different story, with average UK private rents increasing by 3.3% in the year to May – the slowest annual rise since March 2022. Jeremy Leaf, a north London estate agent, notes: "Lettings activity traditionally counterbalances sales, but currently both sectors appear subdued. Landlord concerns about legislative changes are sustaining rents, though cost-of-living pressures are preventing steeper increases."

As the summer market develops, homeowners may wish to note these regional disparities and shifting dynamics when considering their next property move. With inflationary pressures continuing to influence both sales and lettings markets, careful pricing and local market awareness could prove crucial for those planning transactions in the coming months.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

UK House Prices Show Strongest Annual Growth Since 2025

UK property prices recorded their most robust annual increase in over a year during April, rising by 3.8% compared to the same period in 2025, according to the Office for National Statistics. This follows stagnant growth in March and marks the fastest rate of house price inflation since March 2025, just before alterations to Stamp Duty thresholds took effect. The average UK home now stands at £270,000, with regional variations painting a mixed picture of the market.

The North East emerged as the strongest performer, with values climbing 9.9% year-on-year, whilst London continued its downward trend as the weakest region. Property prices in the capital fell by 2.1% over the year to April, extending its run of annual declines to nine consecutive months. Across the nations, average prices reached £291,000 in England, £212,000 in Wales and £192,000 in Scotland. Northern Ireland saw prices rise to £198,000 in Q1 2026 – a 7.4% annual increase.

Experts suggest these figures reflect comparisons with last year's turbulent market conditions during Stamp Duty changes, which temporarily suppressed price growth. Monthly data shows a 0.7% uplift between March and April 2026.

Market Shifts Create Buyer Opportunities

Industry analysts note the current climate presents advantages for prospective purchasers. Richard Donnell of Zoopla observes: "The sales market is softening as we approach summer, with buyer demand 14% lower than this time last year, yet inventory levels remain higher." He emphasises that "serious sellers need to price competitively in what has become a buyers' market," with the North-South divide in pricing and activity persisting.

The rental market tells a different story, with average UK private rents increasing by 3.3% in the year to May – the slowest annual rise since March 2022. Jeremy Leaf, a north London estate agent, notes: "Lettings activity traditionally counterbalances sales, but currently both sectors appear subdued. Landlord concerns about legislative changes are sustaining rents, though cost-of-living pressures are preventing steeper increases."

As the summer market develops, homeowners may wish to note these regional disparities and shifting dynamics when considering their next property move. With inflationary pressures continuing to influence both sales and lettings markets, careful pricing and local market awareness could prove crucial for those planning transactions in the coming months.

item.title: Property giant seals £1bn sale of PRS business in landmark deal
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

L&Q Completes £1bn PRS Business Sale to Morgan Stanley Partnership

Housing association L&Q has finalised the £1.045bn sale of its private rented sector business, Metra Living, to a partnership between Morgan Stanley Real Estate Investing (MSREI) and Ridgeback Group. The landmark transaction forms part of L&Q's strategic shift towards focusing on its core social housing operations whilst improving its financial position.

The deal includes approximately 3,200 rental properties across Greater London, along with the transfer of Metra Living's operational team and around £300m of existing debt facilities. L&Q first revealed plans to divest its PRS arm in November 2024, initiating a competitive bidding process that ultimately selected the MSREI-Ridgeback consortium as preferred buyers.

Strategic Refocus for Housing Association

L&Q's leadership has emphasised how this sale aligns with their long-term objectives of simplifying operations and concentrating resources on social housing provision. The housing association will maintain ties with the business, continuing to provide freeholder services for PRS properties within its developments.

Fiona Fletcher-Smith, L&Q's Group Chief Executive, commented: "This transaction marks a significant step in delivering our strategic vision. Whilst we're proud of building Metra Living into a successful PRS operation since 2015, our focus remains firmly on our social housing mission."

The sale proceeds are expected to bolster L&Q's financial resilience, enabling further investment in new and existing social housing across Greater London and Greater Manchester. Fletcher-Smith added that recent government housing initiatives have created favourable conditions for such strategic moves.

New Owners Bring Global Expertise

Morgan Stanley Real Estate Investing, managing $58bn in global property assets, brings considerable experience to the acquired PRS portfolio. Shamik Narotam, MSREI Managing Director, described Metra Living as "a high-quality, scaled platform in London's supply-constrained market," expressing enthusiasm about building upon its existing foundations.

The transaction represents one of the largest PRS portfolio deals in recent years, highlighting institutional investors' growing appetite for UK rental housing assets. Market analysts suggest such deals could become more frequent as housing associations reassess their operational models amidst changing market conditions and regulatory landscapes.

For prospective homeowners and property investors, this high-profile transaction underscores the continued attractiveness of London's rental market to major financial institutions, potentially influencing future housing supply dynamics in the capital.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

L&Q Completes £1bn PRS Business Sale to Morgan Stanley Partnership

Housing association L&Q has finalised the £1.045bn sale of its private rented sector business, Metra Living, to a partnership between Morgan Stanley Real Estate Investing (MSREI) and Ridgeback Group. The landmark transaction forms part of L&Q's strategic shift towards focusing on its core social housing operations whilst improving its financial position.

The deal includes approximately 3,200 rental properties across Greater London, along with the transfer of Metra Living's operational team and around £300m of existing debt facilities. L&Q first revealed plans to divest its PRS arm in November 2024, initiating a competitive bidding process that ultimately selected the MSREI-Ridgeback consortium as preferred buyers.

Strategic Refocus for Housing Association

L&Q's leadership has emphasised how this sale aligns with their long-term objectives of simplifying operations and concentrating resources on social housing provision. The housing association will maintain ties with the business, continuing to provide freeholder services for PRS properties within its developments.

Fiona Fletcher-Smith, L&Q's Group Chief Executive, commented: "This transaction marks a significant step in delivering our strategic vision. Whilst we're proud of building Metra Living into a successful PRS operation since 2015, our focus remains firmly on our social housing mission."

The sale proceeds are expected to bolster L&Q's financial resilience, enabling further investment in new and existing social housing across Greater London and Greater Manchester. Fletcher-Smith added that recent government housing initiatives have created favourable conditions for such strategic moves.

New Owners Bring Global Expertise

Morgan Stanley Real Estate Investing, managing $58bn in global property assets, brings considerable experience to the acquired PRS portfolio. Shamik Narotam, MSREI Managing Director, described Metra Living as "a high-quality, scaled platform in London's supply-constrained market," expressing enthusiasm about building upon its existing foundations.

The transaction represents one of the largest PRS portfolio deals in recent years, highlighting institutional investors' growing appetite for UK rental housing assets. Market analysts suggest such deals could become more frequent as housing associations reassess their operational models amidst changing market conditions and regulatory landscapes.

For prospective homeowners and property investors, this high-profile transaction underscores the continued attractiveness of London's rental market to major financial institutions, potentially influencing future housing supply dynamics in the capital.

item.title: Cautious response to 3.8% annual house price rise for April
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The latest figures showing a 3.8% annual increase in UK house prices during April have been met with cautious analysis from property market experts. While the headline growth appears positive, industry commentators highlight several factors that may be distorting the true picture of market conditions.

Stamp duty effects may skew figures

Several analysts suggest the apparent price growth could partly reflect temporary distortions from stamp duty changes rather than genuine market strength. The end of certain tax incentives in recent months may have created artificial spikes in certain price brackets as buyers rushed to complete purchases. This temporary boost to higher-value transactions could be inflating the average price figures without representing broader market health.

Market observers note that when examining regional and property type breakdowns, the picture becomes more nuanced. Some areas continue to show resilience while others demonstrate clear signs of cooling demand, particularly in markets that saw the strongest pandemic-era growth.

Underlying market conditions remain subdued

Behind the headline growth figure, industry professionals point to several indicators suggesting softer market fundamentals. Mortgage approval numbers remain below long-term averages, while buyer enquiries and sales volumes continue to show signs of moderation compared to recent years.

  • Higher mortgage rates compared to 2021-22 levels
  • Ongoing cost-of-living pressures affecting buyer budgets
  • Increased price sensitivity among purchasers
  • Longer marketing times in many regions

These factors suggest the market is operating at a different tempo than during the post-pandemic boom period, with both buyers and sellers needing to adjust expectations.

For those considering entering the market, these mixed signals highlight the importance of thorough research and professional advice. While some areas may still offer opportunities, others could present challenges that require careful navigation in the current climate.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The latest figures showing a 3.8% annual increase in UK house prices during April have been met with cautious analysis from property market experts. While the headline growth appears positive, industry commentators highlight several factors that may be distorting the true picture of market conditions.

Stamp duty effects may skew figures

Several analysts suggest the apparent price growth could partly reflect temporary distortions from stamp duty changes rather than genuine market strength. The end of certain tax incentives in recent months may have created artificial spikes in certain price brackets as buyers rushed to complete purchases. This temporary boost to higher-value transactions could be inflating the average price figures without representing broader market health.

Market observers note that when examining regional and property type breakdowns, the picture becomes more nuanced. Some areas continue to show resilience while others demonstrate clear signs of cooling demand, particularly in markets that saw the strongest pandemic-era growth.

Underlying market conditions remain subdued

Behind the headline growth figure, industry professionals point to several indicators suggesting softer market fundamentals. Mortgage approval numbers remain below long-term averages, while buyer enquiries and sales volumes continue to show signs of moderation compared to recent years.

  • Higher mortgage rates compared to 2021-22 levels
  • Ongoing cost-of-living pressures affecting buyer budgets
  • Increased price sensitivity among purchasers
  • Longer marketing times in many regions

These factors suggest the market is operating at a different tempo than during the post-pandemic boom period, with both buyers and sellers needing to adjust expectations.

For those considering entering the market, these mixed signals highlight the importance of thorough research and professional advice. While some areas may still offer opportunities, others could present challenges that require careful navigation in the current climate.

item.title: Latest data ‘as good as it gets’ for property investors
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Property investors find favourable conditions across UK regions

Recent market data suggests property investors may be encountering some of the most advantageous conditions seen in recent years across several UK regions. With softening prices, increased housing stock availability and more measured sales activity, opportunities appear to be emerging for those considering buy-to-let or property investment ventures.

Market shifts create potential openings

The current landscape presents several noteworthy developments for prospective investors:

  • Modest price corrections in certain areas may improve affordability and yield potential
  • Greater availability of properties could provide more choice and negotiating power
  • Slower transaction times might allow for more thorough due diligence processes

Regional variations warrant careful consideration

Whilst the overall picture appears promising, experienced investors typically emphasise the importance of localised research. Market conditions can vary significantly between:

  • Northern regions versus southern hotspots
  • Urban regeneration areas compared to established commuter belts
  • Student accommodation markets versus family rental sectors

Mortgage products for investment properties have also seen adjustments, with some lenders offering more competitive terms for portfolio landlords. Borrowers might find it beneficial to review the latest buy-to-let mortgage deals, as criteria and rates continue to evolve in response to economic conditions.

As always, thorough financial planning and professional advice remain crucial when considering property investment opportunities in the current climate.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Property investors find favourable conditions across UK regions

Recent market data suggests property investors may be encountering some of the most advantageous conditions seen in recent years across several UK regions. With softening prices, increased housing stock availability and more measured sales activity, opportunities appear to be emerging for those considering buy-to-let or property investment ventures.

Market shifts create potential openings

The current landscape presents several noteworthy developments for prospective investors:

  • Modest price corrections in certain areas may improve affordability and yield potential
  • Greater availability of properties could provide more choice and negotiating power
  • Slower transaction times might allow for more thorough due diligence processes

Regional variations warrant careful consideration

Whilst the overall picture appears promising, experienced investors typically emphasise the importance of localised research. Market conditions can vary significantly between:

  • Northern regions versus southern hotspots
  • Urban regeneration areas compared to established commuter belts
  • Student accommodation markets versus family rental sectors

Mortgage products for investment properties have also seen adjustments, with some lenders offering more competitive terms for portfolio landlords. Borrowers might find it beneficial to review the latest buy-to-let mortgage deals, as criteria and rates continue to evolve in response to economic conditions.

As always, thorough financial planning and professional advice remain crucial when considering property investment opportunities in the current climate.

item.title: One in four tenants served notice ahead of RRA
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New research reveals that over 25% of private renters received eviction notices in the months leading up to the abolition of Section 21, as landlords adapted to impending rental reforms. The findings highlight how changes to tenancy laws may be influencing landlord behaviour in the private rental sector.

Landlords respond to regulatory changes

Data suggests many property owners chose to serve notices before the Renters Reform Bill came into effect, with one in four tenants being asked to leave their homes. Industry analysts note this trend may reflect landlords reassessing their portfolios in light of the changing legal landscape.

The phased abolition of Section 21 "no-fault" evictions forms part of wider government plans to rebalance tenant-landlord relationships. Whilst intended to provide greater security for renters, the transition period appears to have prompted some landlords to make pre-emptive decisions about their rental properties.

Impact on the rental market

This wave of notices has contributed to tighter conditions in the private rental sector, with many affected tenants facing challenges finding alternative accommodation. Rental prices in some areas have seen upward pressure as available properties become scarcer.

Prospective renters might consider:

  • Reviewing local market conditions before making decisions
  • Being prepared with references and documentation when applying
  • Exploring longer tenancy agreements where available

For landlords, the changing regulations highlight the importance of staying informed about legal responsibilities and tenant rights. Those maintaining rental properties may wish to review their approach to tenancy management in light of the new rules.

As the rental market continues to adapt to these reforms, both tenants and landlords face a period of adjustment. Understanding the implications of these changes could help all parties navigate the evolving landscape more effectively.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New research reveals that over 25% of private renters received eviction notices in the months leading up to the abolition of Section 21, as landlords adapted to impending rental reforms. The findings highlight how changes to tenancy laws may be influencing landlord behaviour in the private rental sector.

Landlords respond to regulatory changes

Data suggests many property owners chose to serve notices before the Renters Reform Bill came into effect, with one in four tenants being asked to leave their homes. Industry analysts note this trend may reflect landlords reassessing their portfolios in light of the changing legal landscape.

The phased abolition of Section 21 "no-fault" evictions forms part of wider government plans to rebalance tenant-landlord relationships. Whilst intended to provide greater security for renters, the transition period appears to have prompted some landlords to make pre-emptive decisions about their rental properties.

Impact on the rental market

This wave of notices has contributed to tighter conditions in the private rental sector, with many affected tenants facing challenges finding alternative accommodation. Rental prices in some areas have seen upward pressure as available properties become scarcer.

Prospective renters might consider:

  • Reviewing local market conditions before making decisions
  • Being prepared with references and documentation when applying
  • Exploring longer tenancy agreements where available

For landlords, the changing regulations highlight the importance of staying informed about legal responsibilities and tenant rights. Those maintaining rental properties may wish to review their approach to tenancy management in light of the new rules.

As the rental market continues to adapt to these reforms, both tenants and landlords face a period of adjustment. Understanding the implications of these changes could help all parties navigate the evolving landscape more effectively.

item.title: Cost of rental voids up 12.9% this year
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Landlords across England are facing steeper costs when properties sit empty between tenancies, with new figures showing a 12.9% annual increase in rental void expenses. This sharp rise could prompt property investors to reassess their strategies for minimising vacant periods in an increasingly challenging lettings market.

Understanding the rising costs of void periods

Void periods occur when rental properties remain unoccupied between tenancies, during which landlords still face mortgage payments, council tax, and maintenance costs without rental income to offset them. The latest data suggests these financial pressures have intensified significantly over the past year, with the average cost of voids now 12.9% higher than in 2022.

Several factors may be contributing to this increase:

  • Higher mortgage rates increasing monthly holding costs
  • Growing maintenance and utility expenses
  • Longer void periods in some regions as tenant demand fluctuates
  • Increased regulatory compliance costs between tenancies

Strategies landlords might consider

With void costs mounting, property investors may wish to explore approaches to reduce unoccupied periods. Some options could include:

  • Reviewing rental pricing strategies to remain competitive in local markets
  • Considering flexible tenancy terms to attract a wider tenant pool
  • Investing in property presentation and maintenance between tenancies
  • Working with local letting agents who have strong tenant networks
  • Exploring rent guarantee insurance products

Whilst void periods are an inevitable part of property investment, careful planning and market awareness might help landlords mitigate their financial impact. The current climate suggests landlords could benefit from regularly reviewing their void period management strategies as costs continue to rise.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Landlords across England are facing steeper costs when properties sit empty between tenancies, with new figures showing a 12.9% annual increase in rental void expenses. This sharp rise could prompt property investors to reassess their strategies for minimising vacant periods in an increasingly challenging lettings market.

Understanding the rising costs of void periods

Void periods occur when rental properties remain unoccupied between tenancies, during which landlords still face mortgage payments, council tax, and maintenance costs without rental income to offset them. The latest data suggests these financial pressures have intensified significantly over the past year, with the average cost of voids now 12.9% higher than in 2022.

Several factors may be contributing to this increase:

  • Higher mortgage rates increasing monthly holding costs
  • Growing maintenance and utility expenses
  • Longer void periods in some regions as tenant demand fluctuates
  • Increased regulatory compliance costs between tenancies

Strategies landlords might consider

With void costs mounting, property investors may wish to explore approaches to reduce unoccupied periods. Some options could include:

  • Reviewing rental pricing strategies to remain competitive in local markets
  • Considering flexible tenancy terms to attract a wider tenant pool
  • Investing in property presentation and maintenance between tenancies
  • Working with local letting agents who have strong tenant networks
  • Exploring rent guarantee insurance products

Whilst void periods are an inevitable part of property investment, careful planning and market awareness might help landlords mitigate their financial impact. The current climate suggests landlords could benefit from regularly reviewing their void period management strategies as costs continue to rise.

item.title: UK housing market failing to kick into gear
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK housing market remains in a state of subdued activity, with stable property prices failing to mask the ongoing challenges of higher mortgage rates and stretched affordability. Recent data suggests the market is struggling to gain momentum as buyers and sellers adopt a cautious approach amidst economic uncertainty.

Mortgage costs continue to weigh on demand

Despite some stabilisation in house prices across many regions, the cumulative impact of 14 consecutive Bank of England base rate rises continues to influence buyer behaviour. The average two-year fixed mortgage rate, while down from its 2023 peak, remains significantly higher than the ultra-low rates seen during the pandemic years.

First-time buyers in particular face considerable hurdles, with lenders typically requiring larger deposits and stricter affordability checks. Many prospective homeowners might find their borrowing capacity reduced compared to previous years, potentially limiting their property options.

Regional variations in market performance

The national picture conceals important regional differences. While London and the South East have seen more pronounced price adjustments, some northern cities continue to demonstrate relative resilience. Areas with stronger employment markets and more affordable housing stock appear to be weathering the current conditions better than premium property markets.

Estate agents report that correctly priced properties in desirable locations are still attracting interest, though transaction volumes remain below long-term averages. Sellers may need to temper expectations and consider more competitive pricing strategies to attract buyers in the current climate.

For those considering entering the market, it could be worth exploring mortgage options thoroughly and seeking independent financial advice. With lenders periodically adjusting their product ranges, staying informed about the latest deals might help secure more favourable terms.

As the market navigates these challenging conditions, many industry observers suggest the coming months could prove crucial in determining whether the current stability develops into sustained recovery or gives way to further adjustment.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK housing market remains in a state of subdued activity, with stable property prices failing to mask the ongoing challenges of higher mortgage rates and stretched affordability. Recent data suggests the market is struggling to gain momentum as buyers and sellers adopt a cautious approach amidst economic uncertainty.

Mortgage costs continue to weigh on demand

Despite some stabilisation in house prices across many regions, the cumulative impact of 14 consecutive Bank of England base rate rises continues to influence buyer behaviour. The average two-year fixed mortgage rate, while down from its 2023 peak, remains significantly higher than the ultra-low rates seen during the pandemic years.

First-time buyers in particular face considerable hurdles, with lenders typically requiring larger deposits and stricter affordability checks. Many prospective homeowners might find their borrowing capacity reduced compared to previous years, potentially limiting their property options.

Regional variations in market performance

The national picture conceals important regional differences. While London and the South East have seen more pronounced price adjustments, some northern cities continue to demonstrate relative resilience. Areas with stronger employment markets and more affordable housing stock appear to be weathering the current conditions better than premium property markets.

Estate agents report that correctly priced properties in desirable locations are still attracting interest, though transaction volumes remain below long-term averages. Sellers may need to temper expectations and consider more competitive pricing strategies to attract buyers in the current climate.

For those considering entering the market, it could be worth exploring mortgage options thoroughly and seeking independent financial advice. With lenders periodically adjusting their product ranges, staying informed about the latest deals might help secure more favourable terms.

As the market navigates these challenging conditions, many industry observers suggest the coming months could prove crucial in determining whether the current stability develops into sustained recovery or gives way to further adjustment.

item.title: Housing market slowdown deepens as sales fall sharply
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK housing market shows resilience despite sales slowdown

The UK property market has seen a noticeable dip in sales agreed volumes, with May figures showing an 8.1% year-on-year decline according to data from TwentyEA. The firm recorded 109,922 sales agreed (SSTCs) during the month, down from 119,607 in May 2025. This softening in demand has prompted analysts to revise their full-year transactions forecast downwards from 1.2 million to 1.13 million.

Whilst this would represent a 6.8% decline compared with 2025, it's worth noting that activity remains 2.6% above 2024 levels. Market observers suggest several factors may be influencing buyer caution, including economic uncertainty, higher energy prices, inflation concerns and shifting expectations around mortgage rates. The slowdown appears widespread, affecting all price brackets across the UK, with stamp duty changes introduced last April further complicating year-on-year comparisons.

Regional variations and supply dynamics

The market contraction hasn't been evenly distributed geographically. Inner London experienced the most pronounced decline, with sales agreed falling 11.2% year-on-year, followed by the North West at 6.7%. Scotland stands as the exception, showing stable demand unaffected by stamp duty adjustments.

Interestingly, whilst demand has softened, housing supply continues to grow robustly. TwentyEA recorded 794,210 new instructions in the year to May - a record high and 2.7% above the same period last year. March proved particularly active, with over 175,000 new listings entering the market. This growth spans all price segments, with the most notable increase (3.9%) seen in properties priced below £200,000.

Nick Huntley, TwentyEA director, commented: "The market remains fundamentally resilient despite these headwinds. Demand still sits nearly 15% above 2023 levels and continues to outperform 2024 figures, demonstrating the determination of committed buyers even amidst affordability pressures."

For those considering entering the market, these mixed signals suggest opportunities may exist alongside challenges. The increased supply could provide more choice for determined buyers, whilst the cooling demand might create slightly less competitive conditions in some areas. As always, consulting a qualified mortgage adviser could help navigate these changing market dynamics.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK housing market shows resilience despite sales slowdown

The UK property market has seen a noticeable dip in sales agreed volumes, with May figures showing an 8.1% year-on-year decline according to data from TwentyEA. The firm recorded 109,922 sales agreed (SSTCs) during the month, down from 119,607 in May 2025. This softening in demand has prompted analysts to revise their full-year transactions forecast downwards from 1.2 million to 1.13 million.

Whilst this would represent a 6.8% decline compared with 2025, it's worth noting that activity remains 2.6% above 2024 levels. Market observers suggest several factors may be influencing buyer caution, including economic uncertainty, higher energy prices, inflation concerns and shifting expectations around mortgage rates. The slowdown appears widespread, affecting all price brackets across the UK, with stamp duty changes introduced last April further complicating year-on-year comparisons.

Regional variations and supply dynamics

The market contraction hasn't been evenly distributed geographically. Inner London experienced the most pronounced decline, with sales agreed falling 11.2% year-on-year, followed by the North West at 6.7%. Scotland stands as the exception, showing stable demand unaffected by stamp duty adjustments.

Interestingly, whilst demand has softened, housing supply continues to grow robustly. TwentyEA recorded 794,210 new instructions in the year to May - a record high and 2.7% above the same period last year. March proved particularly active, with over 175,000 new listings entering the market. This growth spans all price segments, with the most notable increase (3.9%) seen in properties priced below £200,000.

Nick Huntley, TwentyEA director, commented: "The market remains fundamentally resilient despite these headwinds. Demand still sits nearly 15% above 2023 levels and continues to outperform 2024 figures, demonstrating the determination of committed buyers even amidst affordability pressures."

For those considering entering the market, these mixed signals suggest opportunities may exist alongside challenges. The increased supply could provide more choice for determined buyers, whilst the cooling demand might create slightly less competitive conditions in some areas. As always, consulting a qualified mortgage adviser could help navigate these changing market dynamics.

item.title: Property sales market stalls as lettings activity accelerates
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Recent industry data reveals a growing divergence between the UK's rental and sales markets, with lettings activity accelerating whilst property sales show signs of softening. The latest figures from Propertymark highlight contrasting conditions in these two sectors, with rental demand significantly outstripping supply whilst the sales market demonstrates more measured activity.

Rental Market Remains Highly Competitive

The lettings sector continues to experience intense competition, with Propertymark member agents reporting an average of 86 prospective tenant registrations per branch in April. This strong demand comes against a backdrop of limited supply, with just 12.65 rental properties typically available per branch. The imbalance is further evidenced by the high number of viewings per property, averaging 12.3 for each available rental home.

Void periods remain notably short at three weeks on average, whilst branches reported agreeing 9.63 new tenancies during the month. These figures suggest the rental market continues to favour landlords, with tenants facing competitive conditions when searching for accommodation.

Sales Market Shows More Subdued Activity

In contrast, the sales market appears more balanced, with member branches holding an average of 43 properties for sale during April. Whilst this represents a slight increase in stock levels, buyer activity has softened slightly compared to previous months. The average number of viewings per available property fell to 2.14 in April, down from previous levels.

Market appraisal activity remained steady at 22 valuations per branch, indicating a continued pipeline of potential new instructions. However, the time taken to complete transactions remains lengthy, with many sales taking over 17 weeks to reach exchange. Affordability pressures and mortgage rates continue to influence buyer behaviour, potentially contributing to the more cautious approach in the sales market.

Nathan Emerson, CEO of Propertymark, commented: "The housing market continues to demonstrate resilience despite economic challenges. Whilst viewing levels have softened, buyer registrations and sales agreed remain broadly stable, suggesting committed purchasers are still progressing transactions."

As the market evolves, homeowners and prospective buyers may wish to carefully consider their options, particularly in light of ongoing affordability considerations. The contrasting conditions between the rental and sales markets highlight the importance of seeking professional advice tailored to individual circumstances.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Recent industry data reveals a growing divergence between the UK's rental and sales markets, with lettings activity accelerating whilst property sales show signs of softening. The latest figures from Propertymark highlight contrasting conditions in these two sectors, with rental demand significantly outstripping supply whilst the sales market demonstrates more measured activity.

Rental Market Remains Highly Competitive

The lettings sector continues to experience intense competition, with Propertymark member agents reporting an average of 86 prospective tenant registrations per branch in April. This strong demand comes against a backdrop of limited supply, with just 12.65 rental properties typically available per branch. The imbalance is further evidenced by the high number of viewings per property, averaging 12.3 for each available rental home.

Void periods remain notably short at three weeks on average, whilst branches reported agreeing 9.63 new tenancies during the month. These figures suggest the rental market continues to favour landlords, with tenants facing competitive conditions when searching for accommodation.

Sales Market Shows More Subdued Activity

In contrast, the sales market appears more balanced, with member branches holding an average of 43 properties for sale during April. Whilst this represents a slight increase in stock levels, buyer activity has softened slightly compared to previous months. The average number of viewings per available property fell to 2.14 in April, down from previous levels.

Market appraisal activity remained steady at 22 valuations per branch, indicating a continued pipeline of potential new instructions. However, the time taken to complete transactions remains lengthy, with many sales taking over 17 weeks to reach exchange. Affordability pressures and mortgage rates continue to influence buyer behaviour, potentially contributing to the more cautious approach in the sales market.

Nathan Emerson, CEO of Propertymark, commented: "The housing market continues to demonstrate resilience despite economic challenges. Whilst viewing levels have softened, buyer registrations and sales agreed remain broadly stable, suggesting committed purchasers are still progressing transactions."

As the market evolves, homeowners and prospective buyers may wish to carefully consider their options, particularly in light of ongoing affordability considerations. The contrasting conditions between the rental and sales markets highlight the importance of seeking professional advice tailored to individual circumstances.

item.title: BTL landlords eye exit despite rising rental returns
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New research suggests a growing number of buy-to-let landlords are contemplating reducing their property portfolios, despite rising rental yields across the UK market. The findings highlight ongoing challenges in the private rental sector, where regulatory pressures appear to be outweighing financial incentives for many investors.

Landlords weigh up portfolio reductions

A recent study by Aldermore Bank reveals that 42% of landlords are considering scaling back their property holdings. This potential exodus comes at a time when rental demand continues to outstrip supply, with nearly half (47%) of surveyed landlords reporting improved yields over the past year. Average returns have increased by 7.2%, while 18% of investors saw yields rise by 10% or more.

However, financial gains alone aren’t convincing landlords to expand their portfolios. Some 45% stated current market conditions are preventing further investment, suggesting that appetite for buy-to-let properties remains subdued. The research indicates a clear mismatch between strong tenant demand and investor confidence in the sector’s future.

Regulatory pressures dominate concerns

For those considering leaving the market, regulatory changes emerged as the primary concern, cited by 43% of landlords. Recent legislation such as the Renters’ Rights Act appears to be influencing investment decisions, alongside tax changes (39%) and rising maintenance costs (37%). More than half (55%) said additional tax increases on property-related income could prompt a complete exit from the market.

The findings also reveal growing frustration among landlords, with 30% feeling unfairly blamed for systemic housing issues. Industry experts warn this sentiment, combined with ongoing policy changes, could further reduce investment in private rentals – potentially exacerbating the existing supply-demand imbalance.

Jon Cooper, Aldermore’s mortgages director, noted: “While tenant demand remains robust and yields are improving, many landlords feel hesitant about committing further capital. Maintaining a healthy private rental sector requires confidence among property investors to provide quality accommodation.”

As the buy-to-let landscape continues to evolve, landlords may wish to review their portfolios carefully and seek professional advice to navigate changing market conditions. With regulatory pressures unlikely to ease, the coming months could prove decisive for the sector’s future direction.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New research suggests a growing number of buy-to-let landlords are contemplating reducing their property portfolios, despite rising rental yields across the UK market. The findings highlight ongoing challenges in the private rental sector, where regulatory pressures appear to be outweighing financial incentives for many investors.

Landlords weigh up portfolio reductions

A recent study by Aldermore Bank reveals that 42% of landlords are considering scaling back their property holdings. This potential exodus comes at a time when rental demand continues to outstrip supply, with nearly half (47%) of surveyed landlords reporting improved yields over the past year. Average returns have increased by 7.2%, while 18% of investors saw yields rise by 10% or more.

However, financial gains alone aren’t convincing landlords to expand their portfolios. Some 45% stated current market conditions are preventing further investment, suggesting that appetite for buy-to-let properties remains subdued. The research indicates a clear mismatch between strong tenant demand and investor confidence in the sector’s future.

Regulatory pressures dominate concerns

For those considering leaving the market, regulatory changes emerged as the primary concern, cited by 43% of landlords. Recent legislation such as the Renters’ Rights Act appears to be influencing investment decisions, alongside tax changes (39%) and rising maintenance costs (37%). More than half (55%) said additional tax increases on property-related income could prompt a complete exit from the market.

The findings also reveal growing frustration among landlords, with 30% feeling unfairly blamed for systemic housing issues. Industry experts warn this sentiment, combined with ongoing policy changes, could further reduce investment in private rentals – potentially exacerbating the existing supply-demand imbalance.

Jon Cooper, Aldermore’s mortgages director, noted: “While tenant demand remains robust and yields are improving, many landlords feel hesitant about committing further capital. Maintaining a healthy private rental sector requires confidence among property investors to provide quality accommodation.”

As the buy-to-let landscape continues to evolve, landlords may wish to review their portfolios carefully and seek professional advice to navigate changing market conditions. With regulatory pressures unlikely to ease, the coming months could prove decisive for the sector’s future direction.

item.title: Retirement crisis hiding in Britain’s housing wealth
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Retirement income gap persists despite homeowners' property wealth

New research reveals millions of older homeowners face retirement incomes below recommended living standards, despite sitting on substantial housing wealth. The findings highlight a growing disparity between property assets and pension provisions, with nearly half of homeowners aged 55-79 expected to struggle financially in later life.

Housing wealth versus pension shortfalls

A recent report from Fairer Finance suggests 3.7 million homeowner households in the 55-79 age group may have retirement incomes falling short of the UK moderate living standard. This represents 46% of homeowners in this demographic, many of whom hold property assets worth between £200,000 and £400,000.
