The analysis indicates single female homeowners are particularly vulnerable, with 65% projected to miss the income benchmark despite average housing wealth of £225,000. For single male homeowners, the figure stands at 44%, while 37% of homeowner couples face similar challenges despite typically owning properties worth £275,000.

Equity release awareness versus uptake

The research reveals a notable gap between awareness and utilisation of housing wealth solutions. Whilst 70% of homeowners aged 55-79 know about equity release products, only 13% have seriously considered a lifetime mortgage. Most respondents indicated they would rather reduce spending or alter their lifestyle than tap into property wealth.

Key findings include:

  • 1.8 million households with income shortfalls hold £200,000-£400,000 in housing wealth
  • 650,000 homeowners have property assets exceeding £400,000
  • Women express greater concerns about retirement finances than men
  • Downsizing remains more popular than equity release for addressing shortfalls

These findings underscore the complex relationship between property ownership and retirement planning in the UK. With many homeowners potentially overlooking housing wealth as part of their retirement strategy, financial advisers might highlight the importance of considering all assets when planning for later life.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Retirement income gap persists despite homeowners' property wealth

New research reveals millions of older homeowners face retirement incomes below recommended living standards, despite sitting on substantial housing wealth. The findings highlight a growing disparity between property assets and pension provisions, with nearly half of homeowners aged 55-79 expected to struggle financially in later life.

Housing wealth versus pension shortfalls

A recent report from Fairer Finance suggests 3.7 million homeowner households in the 55-79 age group may have retirement incomes falling short of the UK moderate living standard. This represents 46% of homeowners in this demographic, many of whom hold property assets worth between £200,000 and £400,000.

The analysis indicates single female homeowners are particularly vulnerable, with 65% projected to miss the income benchmark despite average housing wealth of £225,000. For single male homeowners, the figure stands at 44%, while 37% of homeowner couples face similar challenges despite typically owning properties worth £275,000.

Equity release awareness versus uptake

The research reveals a notable gap between awareness and utilisation of housing wealth solutions. Whilst 70% of homeowners aged 55-79 know about equity release products, only 13% have seriously considered a lifetime mortgage. Most respondents indicated they would rather reduce spending or alter their lifestyle than tap into property wealth.

Key findings include:

  • 1.8 million households with income shortfalls hold £200,000-£400,000 in housing wealth
  • 650,000 homeowners have property assets exceeding £400,000
  • Women express greater concerns about retirement finances than men
  • Downsizing remains more popular than equity release for addressing shortfalls

These findings underscore the complex relationship between property ownership and retirement planning in the UK. With many homeowners potentially overlooking housing wealth as part of their retirement strategy, financial advisers might highlight the importance of considering all assets when planning for later life.

item.title: Chestertons boosts Caribbean presence
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

International estate agency Chestertons has extended its global reach with two new strategic partnerships in the Caribbean property market. The firm has established operations in St Vincent and the Grenadines alongside the Cayman Islands, marking a significant expansion of its overseas network.

Strategic Caribbean expansion

The move forms part of Chestertons' ongoing strategy to strengthen its presence in key international property markets. By partnering with established local agencies in these Caribbean locations, the company gains immediate access to prime real estate markets favoured by British investors and second-home buyers.

St Vincent and the Grenadines offers a growing luxury property sector, whilst the Cayman Islands remains one of the most stable Caribbean markets with strong demand from overseas buyers. This dual expansion allows Chestertons to cater to different segments of the international property market.

Opportunities for UK investors

For UK-based property investors considering overseas opportunities, Chestertons' expansion could provide:

  • Improved access to Caribbean property markets through a familiar UK brand
  • Local market expertise combined with international brokerage experience
  • Potential for portfolio diversification in stable, dollar-pegged economies

Those exploring international property investment might wish to consider that Caribbean markets often offer different risk profiles and tax implications compared to UK buy-to-let investments. Professional financial advice is typically recommended when purchasing property abroad.

As global property markets continue to evolve, established UK agencies expanding their international networks may provide British investors with more options for overseas real estate opportunities. The Caribbean remains a popular choice for those seeking holiday homes or investment properties in favourable climates.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

International estate agency Chestertons has extended its global reach with two new strategic partnerships in the Caribbean property market. The firm has established operations in St Vincent and the Grenadines alongside the Cayman Islands, marking a significant expansion of its overseas network.

Strategic Caribbean expansion

The move forms part of Chestertons' ongoing strategy to strengthen its presence in key international property markets. By partnering with established local agencies in these Caribbean locations, the company gains immediate access to prime real estate markets favoured by British investors and second-home buyers.

St Vincent and the Grenadines offers a growing luxury property sector, whilst the Cayman Islands remains one of the most stable Caribbean markets with strong demand from overseas buyers. This dual expansion allows Chestertons to cater to different segments of the international property market.

Opportunities for UK investors

For UK-based property investors considering overseas opportunities, Chestertons' expansion could provide:

  • Improved access to Caribbean property markets through a familiar UK brand
  • Local market expertise combined with international brokerage experience
  • Potential for portfolio diversification in stable, dollar-pegged economies

Those exploring international property investment might wish to consider that Caribbean markets often offer different risk profiles and tax implications compared to UK buy-to-let investments. Professional financial advice is typically recommended when purchasing property abroad.

As global property markets continue to evolve, established UK agencies expanding their international networks may provide British investors with more options for overseas real estate opportunities. The Caribbean remains a popular choice for those seeking holiday homes or investment properties in favourable climates.

item.title: Egypt overlooked by property investors
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Egypt's property market may be flying under the radar for many UK investors, despite showing promising signs of growth potential. With comparatively lower entry prices and a rapidly expanding population, the North African country could present an alternative opportunity for those looking to diversify their property portfolios internationally.

Why Egypt's property market merits attention

Several factors make Egypt's real estate sector worthy of consideration:

  • Property prices remain relatively affordable compared to many established markets
  • The country's young and growing population continues to drive housing demand
  • Major infrastructure projects are transforming urban areas
  • The government has introduced reforms to encourage foreign investment

Important considerations for potential investors

Whilst the Egyptian market shows potential, UK investors might want to carefully weigh several aspects before committing:

  • Currency fluctuations between the Egyptian pound and sterling could affect returns
  • Local property laws and taxation systems differ significantly from the UK
  • Political and economic stability should be thoroughly researched
  • Practicalities of managing overseas properties may require local partnerships

As with any international property investment, thorough due diligence and professional advice remain essential. The Egyptian market's current affordability and demographic trends could make it worth monitoring for investors seeking emerging market opportunities beyond traditional European destinations.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Egypt's property market may be flying under the radar for many UK investors, despite showing promising signs of growth potential. With comparatively lower entry prices and a rapidly expanding population, the North African country could present an alternative opportunity for those looking to diversify their property portfolios internationally.

Why Egypt's property market merits attention

Several factors make Egypt's real estate sector worthy of consideration:

  • Property prices remain relatively affordable compared to many established markets
  • The country's young and growing population continues to drive housing demand
  • Major infrastructure projects are transforming urban areas
  • The government has introduced reforms to encourage foreign investment

Important considerations for potential investors

Whilst the Egyptian market shows potential, UK investors might want to carefully weigh several aspects before committing:

  • Currency fluctuations between the Egyptian pound and sterling could affect returns
  • Local property laws and taxation systems differ significantly from the UK
  • Political and economic stability should be thoroughly researched
  • Practicalities of managing overseas properties may require local partnerships

As with any international property investment, thorough due diligence and professional advice remain essential. The Egyptian market's current affordability and demographic trends could make it worth monitoring for investors seeking emerging market opportunities beyond traditional European destinations.
